name: Gankster
description: An all-in-one competitive gaming platform (LFG, scrims, ladders and team tools) that connects players and teams across titles like Valorant, LoL, Rocket League and Dota 2.

details.business: B2B/B2C platform (consumer product with team/org use)
details.targetMarket: Competitive players, amateur/pro esports teams, collegiate & community orgs, tournament organizers

items: Gankster Groups — persistent duo/team posts and recommended groups
Built-in scrim chat and automatic invite/party actions via game integration
Overwolf in-game overlay / client integration for automatic invites and match-sync
Match data tracking and basic leaderboards/ladders
Community/teams pages, weekly/monthly rewards and leaderboards
Public blog, guides and active Discord community

missingFeatures: Dedicated 1:1 coaching marketplace and coach booking workflow (searchable coach profiles, credential verification, escrow/payments, recurring sessions) — Gankster focuses on team LFG/scrims rather than paid coaching discovery.

Advanced individual performance analytics and VOD review tools for coaches (timelineed highlights, heatmaps, automated clip generation) — match data is tracked but appears team/scrim-focused and lightweight for coach workflows.

Clear, public pricing and packaged team subscription tiers — site terms mention subscription plans but there is no transparent pricing page for teams/orgs, which raises buyer friction for organizations seeking to compare plans.

Verified credentialing / trust layer for paid services (coach certifications, background checks, formal guarantees) — reputation is peer-based and useful for matchmaking but doesn't substitute for coach credential verification needed for higher-ticket purchases.

Enterprise/API/white-label offerings for publishers or large orgs (SSO, data export, API access) — integrations emphasize overlays and Discord, but there is limited public info about B2B APIs or enterprise controls.

pricingPlans.name: Free
pricingPlans.note: Core LFG, scrim scheduling, reputation, and community features are free for players and teams; monetization noted to be introduced via app/subscriptions.

pricingPlans.name: Team / Subscription (in-app)
pricingPlans.note: Gankster mentions subscription plans in its Terms and has signaled monetization plans; pricing is surfaced inside the app rather than on a public pricing page.

pricingEvidence.label: Terms of Service (subscription mention)
pricingEvidence.claim: Terms state access to Services and features may be subject to payment and reference Subscription Plans priced inside the app.

pricingEvidence.label: GamesBeat (monetization roadmap)
pricingEvidence.claim: Article notes Gankster planned to begin monetizing in Q1 2023 and will offer paid plans/features.

companyEvidence.label: GamesBeat (seed funding)
companyEvidence.claim: Gankster raised a $4.25M seed round co-led by Bessemer Venture Partners and Hetz Ventures.

companyEvidence.label: Esports Insider (funding & founding year)
companyEvidence.claim: Coverage states Gankster was founded in 2021 and raised $4.25M to expand its competitive gaming platform.

companyEvidence.label: Tracxn / Crunchbase summaries
companyEvidence.claim: Company profiles list Gankster as a Tel Aviv-based startup founded in 2021 with a Seed round of ~$4.25M.

reviewEvidence.label: Reddit (community reaction / traction)
reviewEvidence.claim: Multiple Reddit posts from Gankster team and community show early traction, user praise for LFG/scrim features, and anecdotal metrics (thousands of users and scrims).

reviewEvidence.label: Overwolf app listing
reviewEvidence.claim: Overwolf describes Gankster's LFG and reputation features and shows the product is distributed as an in-game overlay, with user reviews on that platform.

reviewEvidence.label: Discord community invite (size)
reviewEvidence.claim: Public Discord invite lists a large community (100k+ members in invite link metadata) demonstrating active user community and engagement.

featureEvidence.label: Gankster product pages
featureEvidence.claim: Homepage and feature pages describe LFG, Groups, scrim scheduling, reputation system, leaderboards and team tools.

featureEvidence.label: Gankster Find Valorant players page
featureEvidence.claim: Describes the Valorant-specific LFG tool, matching, chat, queue-together and mobile app integration.

featureEvidence.label: GamesIndustry / Esports Insiders coverage
featureEvidence.claim: Press notes the reputation system, scrim scheduling, leaderboards and Discord integration as core product features.

strengths: Deep focus on competitive workflows (LFG -> scrims -> ladders) that matches the needs of amateur and collegiate teams rather than generic social matchmaking.
Integrated reputation system (peer post-match reviews) that helps reduce no-shows/toxicity and increases reliability for team scrims.
Strong early traction and VC backing (Bessemer, Hetz) — gives credibility and runway to expand into more games and features.
Game-client integrations (Overwolf/in-game actions, automatic invites) and Discord tie-ins reduce friction when moving from scheduling to playing.
weaknesses: No clear public pricing or packaged team plans — buyers (team managers/orgs) face friction when evaluating and budgeting for paid features.
Lacks a dedicated coaching marketplace and coach-specific tooling (scheduling escrow, recurring lessons, coach verification) — missed revenue vertical and product expansion opportunity.
Analytics and coach-oriented review/VOD tooling appear limited compared with specialized coaching platforms; this hinders higher-value coaching engagements.
Heavy reliance on game integrations (Overwolf, API access) could slow expansion to non-PC or publisher-restricted titles and complicate scaling to mobile-first esports.
